patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out lately, and my eyes look a little yellow.” Volunteer early: 2 weeks of worsening fatigue/weakness, yellow eyes worse in mornings, shortness of breath on stairs, dull heaviness under left ribs, and that past blood tests showed “different shapes and sizes” of cells. If asked, add: dark/tea-colored urine at times, headaches/lightheaded standing, heart racing with walking, easy bruising, fatigue after small tasks, similar episodes after colds, pale skin, and crampy belly pain after greasy meals. Do not mention unless specifically asked: prior gallstones/possible gallstones, low-grade fevers after viral illnesses with more yellowing, being told your spleen is big, leg cramps/dehydration, iron pills didn’t help. Be reluctant to bring up yellowing (fear of being judged for drinking), low sex drive/looking “gray,” and skipping meals/extra coffee—share only if the clinician asks gently. Voice misconceptions: worry about hepatitis, started an herbal “liver cleanse,” wonder about iron deficiency (ice craving), think left pain might be reflux/ulcer. If shown empathy, soften, admit worries about cost, genetic testing, and missing work.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 task, the benchmark outcome is correct recognition and documentation of trigeminal neuralgia based on classic history (paroxysmal, electric-shock facial pain with triggers) and exclusion of key red flags.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "trigeminal neuralgia" and explains it in patient-friendly terms (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis includes the words "trigeminal neuralgia").

Elicits and documents key diagnostic features: brief, recurrent paroxysms of unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution; severe electric shock/stabbing quality; triggerable by light touch/chewing/talking/toothbrushing; pain-free intervals.
Assesses laterality and distribution (V1/V2/V3) and notes that V1 involvement raises need for careful evaluation but does not change the L0 requirement to record diagnosis.

Screens for red flags/secondary causes and atypical features: persistent pain between attacks, sensory loss/numbness, bilateral symptoms, age <40, progressive neurologic deficits, hearing loss/vertigo, constitutional symptoms, history of MS or tumor, dental/sinus pathology signs.

Performs or describes a focused cranial nerve/neurologic exam expectation (at least asks about facial numbness/weakness, corneal reflex symptoms, and other neurologic symptoms) and notes that abnormal findings suggest secondary trigeminal neuralgia and need for further evaluation.

Provides a brief differential in narrative (even if not recorded via tool): dental pain, temporomandibular disorder, cluster headache, migraine, sinusitis, postherpetic neuralgia, glossopharyngeal neuralgia.

Does not prescribe medications in the L0 benchmark outcome; may mention that first-line treatment is carbamazepine as part of education (communication milestone: explain_treatment includes the word "carbamazepine") without issuing a prescription tool call.

If discussing future treatment, explicitly mentions that before prescribing carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine the clinician must check allergies, contraindications, and drug interactions (required safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, drug_interaction_check).

Gives safety-net advice: seek urgent care for new neurologic deficits, persistent/worsening pain pattern, vision changes, fever, facial swelling, or inability to eat/drink; advises follow-up for reassessment if symptoms change.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and briefly distinguishes it from normal short sleep or transient stress-related sleep disturbance.
Assesses insomnia pattern and severity: sleep-onset vs sleep-maintenance vs early-morning awakening, duration, frequency, daytime impairment, and patient goals.

Screens for key contributors/comorbidities that change management: depression/suicidality, anxiety, substance/alcohol/cannabis use, stimulant/caffeine use, medications that disrupt sleep, pain, GERD, nocturia, pregnancy/postpartum status, and shift work/circadian misalignment.

Screens for sleep apnea and other sleep disorders when suggested (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, restless legs symptoms), and avoids sedative-hypnotics if high suspicion for untreated OSA without appropriate evaluation.
Provides first-line treatment counseling including CBT-I (explicitly named) and core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive therapy, relaxation, sleep hygiene as adjunct).

When discussing pharmacologic options, uses shared decision-making, sets expectations (short-term use, lowest effective dose), and discusses common adverse effects (next-day sedation, cognitive impairment, falls, parasomnias, dependence).

Explicitly mentions "barbiturate" in the treatment explanation and clearly advises against barbiturates for insomnia due to safety risks (overdose, dependence, narrow therapeutic index), unless an exceptional indication is present (generally not for insomnia).

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about drug allergies (allergy_check), reviews current meds/OTC/herbals for interactions (drug_interaction_check), and evaluates contraindications (contraindication_check) such as pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment, respiratory depression/OSA risk, history of substance use disorder, older age/fall risk, and concomitant CNS depressants.
Avoids prescribing contraindicated or high-risk agents (e.g., barbiturates; chronic benzodiazepines; sedatives with concurrent alcohol/opioids) and documents rationale if any sedative is chosen.
Includes a follow-up and monitoring plan in narrative (even if not as a tool): reassess sleep metrics, side effects, adherence to CBT-I, and taper/discontinue sedatives if used.

If medication is appropriate, prescribe a short-term, guideline-consistent option tailored to symptoms and risk profile (e.g., low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance; melatonin/ramelteon for sleep onset; consider non-benzodiazepine hypnotic only if appropriate), with lowest effective dose and limited duration.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "Prader-Willi syndrome" and "genetic," and clarifies it is due to loss of paternal gene expression in the 15q11-q13 region (can be described as imprinting defect/point mutation affecting imprinting center) rather than blaming parental actions.

Explains treatment using the terms "Prader-Willi syndrome" and "growth hormone," including expected benefits (growth, lean mass, function) and key risks/monitoring (sleep-disordered breathing/OSA, glucose intolerance, scoliosis, IGF-1 monitoring).

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: explicitly checks for allergies prior to prescribing and assesses contraindications to growth hormone (especially untreated severe obstructive sleep apnea, active malignancy, acute critical illness); failure to do either is a critical error.
If discussing drug interactions, does not overstate them; acknowledges interaction checks are prudent but not typically limiting for somatropin (non-critical).

Addresses hyperphagia/obesity risk with concrete non-pharmacologic management: structured food environment, calorie supervision, dietitian involvement, and activity plan; avoids prescribing appetite suppressants as first-line.

Recommends appropriate baseline/ongoing evaluations commonly required in PWS care (sleep study/OSA screening, thyroid function, glucose/A1c, scoliosis assessment, developmental/behavioral assessment) and documents follow-up expectations even if not using a follow-up tool.
Uses patient-centered, age-appropriate communication and obtains consent/assent where applicable; avoids stigmatizing language and provides resources/support for caregivers.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose PWS due to point mutation/imprinting defect, start/continue growth hormone with mandatory safety checks, and refer to endocrinology/genetics for multidisciplinary management.
must_collect: sleep symptoms suggestive of OSA (snoring, apneas, daytime somnolence)
past medical history for contraindications (active malignancy, acute critical illness)
steps: Confirm molecular diagnosis context and key clinical features consistent with Prader-Willi syndrome; clarify it is genetic.
Explain diagnosis and anticipated complications (hyperphagia/obesity, endocrine issues, developmental/behavioral concerns).
Before prescribing: perform allergy check; perform contraindication check focusing on untreated severe OSA and other GH contraindications; optionally check drug interactions.
Prescribe/plan recombinant human growth hormone with monitoring plan (IGF-1 targets, growth parameters) and emphasize need for OSA evaluation and ongoing surveillance.
Refer to pediatric endocrinology and genetics (and sleep medicine/ENT if OSA suspected) for initiation/monitoring and comprehensive care coordination.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent defers actual growth hormone initiation pending sleep study/ENT evaluation but still documents GH as indicated therapy and refers urgently to endocrinology for initiation once cleared.
steps: Explain genetic diagnosis of Prader-Willi syndrome and main risks.
Complete allergy and contraindication checks; identify possible OSA risk.
Document GH as indicated but defer start until sleep evaluation completed if OSA suspected/untreated.
Refer to pediatric endocrinology and sleep medicine/ENT; outline monitoring and family education.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes broader multidisciplinary referrals (dietitian, developmental pediatrics/psychology) in addition to endocrinology/genetics while still meeting required tool outcomes.
steps: Explain PWS as a genetic condition and address caregiver questions.
Perform allergy and contraindication checks prior to GH prescribing; optionally check interactions.
Prescribe GH with monitoring and safety counseling.
Refer to pediatric endocrinology/genetics and recommend dietitian + developmental/behavioral supports (may be included in referral reason/notes).
